coredata.dc:title: Boron- and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with enhanced supercapacitance
coredata.dc:creator: Dejpasand, Mohamad Taghi
Sharifi, Samira
Saievar-Iranizad, Esmaiel
Yazdani, Ahmad
Rahimi, Kourosh
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with their very large specific surface area and numerous edges can offer promising novel features as metal-free and inexpensive electrodes of supercapacitors. Here, we investigated the effect of boron and nitrogen doping in GQDs on their electrochemical energy storage properties. The GQDs as well as B-GQDs and N-GQDs were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method and characterized by X-ray diffractometry, UV-Vis absorption spectrophotometry, transmission electron microscopy, and energy-dispersive X-ray mapping techniques. Electrochemical properties of the nanoparticles were investigated in a three-electrode setup using cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic charge/discharge techniques in KOH electrolyte. The N-GQDs electrode showed the highest specific capacitance of 283 F/g at 1 A/g as compared to the GQDs and B-GQDs electrodes. The N-GQDs electrode exhibited the specific energy and specific power of 6.2 Wh/Kg and 108 W/Kg, respectively. The N-GQDs electrode showed also better cycling stability (80% capacity retention) than the GQDs and B-GQDs electrodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Doping
Supercapacitor
Hydrothermal synthesis
Cycling stability

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The procedure to synthesize the samples.
A schematic representation of how Ni foam substrate is coated with the synthesized nanoparticles.
XRD patterns, TEM images, and EDX mappings of the prepared (A, B, C) GQDs, (D, E, F) B-GQDs, and (G, H, I) N-GQDs.
CV curves of (A) the bare glassy carbon electrode, (B) GQDs, (C) B-GQDs, and (D) N-GQDs.
(A) absorption spectra of the samples. Tauc plots of the prepared (B) GQDs, (C) B-GQDs, and (D) N-GQDs.
CV curves of the electrodes constructed from (A) GQDs, (B) B-GQDs, and (C) N-GQDs (at various scan rates). (D) Comparative CV curves of the electrodes at the scan rate of 5 mV/s.
GCD curves of the electrodes constructed from (A) GQDs, (B) B-GQDs, and (C) N-GQDs (at various scan rates). (D) Comparative GCD curves of the electrodes at the current density of 1 A/g.
(A) Charge-discharge cycling stabilities of the samples at the current density of 20 A/g for 2000 GCD cycles. (B) Specific energy versus specific power of the samples. (C) EIS curves of the samples.

(A) CV curves of the N-GQDs//AC asymmetric supercapacitor for different potential windows at the scan rate of 20 mV/s and (B) GCD curves of the supercapacitor at different current densities within the potential window of 0–1.5 V. (C) A picture of the assembled asymmetric supercapacitor lighting up a red LED.
Specific capacitances (F/g) of the electrodes at the current density of 1 A/g.
Summary on the fabricated graphene quantum dot-based supercapacitor devices
Specific energy and specific power of the electrode materials at the current density of 1 A/g

head.ce:title: Boron- and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with enhanced supercapacitance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Condensed Matter Physics Group, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Jalal-Ale-Ahmad Avenue, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Condensed Matter Physics Group, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Jalal-Ale-Ahmad Avenue, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with their very large specific surface area and numerous edges can offer promising novel features as metal-free and inexpensive electrodes of supercapacitors. Here, we investigated the effect of boron and nitrogen doping in GQDs on their electrochemical energy storage properties. The GQDs as well as B-GQDs and N-GQDs were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method and characterized by X-ray diffractometry, UV-Vis absorption spectrophotometry, transmission electron microscopy, and energy-dispersive X-ray mapping techniques. Electrochemical properties of the nanoparticles were investigated in a three-electrode setup using cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic charge/discharge techniques in KOH electrolyte. The N-GQDs electrode showed the highest specific capacitance of 283 F/g at 1 A/g as compared to the GQDs and B-GQDs electrodes. The N-GQDs electrode exhibited the specific energy and specific power of 6.2 Wh/Kg and 108 W/Kg, respectively. The N-GQDs electrode showed also better cycling stability (80% capacity retention) than the GQDs and B-GQDs electrodes.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the limitations of energy supplies, the ever-rising temperatures, and the environmental pollution in the present century, the development of clean and renewable energy sources has received increasing attention. Besides, the low cost, the ease of production, the availability, and the non-toxicity of precursors are very important for the construction of energy-related devices. The fields such as photocatalytic water splitting, photovoltaics, supercapacitors, batteries, diodes, and sensors are being developed at a high speed [1,2]. Supercapacitors can be classified into two basic types according to their charge storage mechanisms: (1) the electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), where the electrode materials consist mainly of carbon-based structures based on the accumulation of ions at the electrode/electrolyte interface, and (2) faradaic capacitors, where the electrode materials consist of metal oxides and conducting polymers, which generate fast reversible redox reactions [3,4] the variable oxidation state and environmental benign nature of the metal oxide brought them as a promising candidate for supercapacitor electrode application. However, poor electrical conductivity and less reactive sites limit their commercialization [5] and carbon-based EDLCs have some problems including low energy density and poor rate performance due to their limited accessible storage sites. As a relatively new member of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanoparticles, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received substantial research attention [6]. The GQD provides the double-layer properties and electrochemical active sites. Furthermore, it can be improving the conductivity of electrode material [7]. GQDs consist of highly crystalline and few atom-thick graphene with sp2 hybridized carbon and with the dimensions less than 100 nm. The thickness of GQD structure is close to the “ultimate limit of dimensions” (a single atom) [8]. GQDs exhibit unique properties due to their strong quantum confinement and edge effects including photoluminescence, high conductivity, chemical inertness, excellent stability, and environmental friendliness [9,10]. Due to the quantum confinement effect, GQDs have a wider energy bandgap and delocalized charge carriers which, along with their high specific surface area and excellent transport capability, result in additional properties [11]. It has been shown that doping nitrogen and oxygen in a carbon matrix can changes its band gab, energy density and specific capacitance [11,12]. Doping of heteroatoms in GQDs has been reported with different dopants like N, Se, S, Cl, F, B, K to yield N-/, S-/, Se-/, Cl-/, F-/, B-/, and K-/ doped GQDs. The introduction of defect energy levels with the doping of heteroatoms alters the band gap from LUMO (π) to HOMO level (π*).Some more work related to co-doping in GQDs with SN/N-S doped GQDs, B, N-/ doped GQDs, N, P-/ doped GQDs. was done to make an improvement in electron density as well as for optimizing the luminescent behavior of GQDs [13,14]. Here, we investigated the facile fabrication of N-doped and B-doped GQDs and their chemical and physical properties. The end studied their application as supercapacitor electrodes.
Glucose (99.5%), citric acid (99.5%), boric acid (99.5%), and urea (99.5%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Co. as precursors.

A facile hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize the nanoparticles. The pH of the samples was kept constant at ∼7. After the synthesis procedure, the obtained samples were dialyzed through a dialysis bag with the molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da to remove the oversized particles. Finally, the concentration of all samples was adjusted to 1 mg/mL for further analysis and electrochemical tests. To synthesize GQDs, 0.02 g glucose was stirred in 20 mL deionized (DI) water at room temperature for 15 min. The obtained solution was put into a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After the procedure, the colorless solution changed to a light yellow one. To synthesize B-GQDs, 48 mg boric acid and 0.2 g glucose were added to 20 mL DI water, and the solution was stirred at room temperature for 1 h. The obtained solution was added into a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 3 h. After the procedure, the colorless solution changed to a very pale yellow one. To synthesize N-GQDs, 0.30 g citric acid and 0.35 g urea were added to 25 mL DI water at room temperature for 1 h. The obtained solution was added into a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After the procedure, the colorless solution changed to an orange one. Fig. 1 shows a schematic of our synthesis procedure [15–18].

Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping images were recorded by a BRUKER XFlash 6 I10 instrument. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured using a Philips CM30 300 kV instrument. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by a Philips X'pert instrument with Cu kα radiation source. The absorption spectra of the samples were recorded using a Perkin-Elmer lambda 25 spectrophotometer. Electrochemical measurements were performed in a three-electrode setup at room temperature by utilizing a VSP-300 Multichannel Potentiostat/Galvanostat/EIS instrument (Bio-Logic Science Instruments).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed in a three-electrode setup. The working electrode was a Ni foam substrate (1 cm2) coated with our samples (see Fig. 2 ). The counter electrode was a platinum sheet (1 cm2, 99.99%) and the reference electrode was Ag/AgCl. The electrolyte was 3 M KOH solution at room temperature. Supercapacitance properties of the synthesized N-GQDs were also studied in an asymmetric two-electrode setup using activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode and N-GQDs as the positive electrode. The AC electrode was fabricated by mixing a 95:5 wt% mixture of AC:PVDF (as a binder) in N-Methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent. The slurry was then coated on a nickel foam substrate by a brush and the obtained electrode was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 10 h with a mass loading of ∼3 mg/cm2 (more details in the following). The electrodes were separated by a filter paper wetted with a 3 M KOH electrolyte.

Fig. 3 shows XRD patterns of the prepared nanoparticles. The broad diffraction peaks at 26.36 ̊, 27.5 ̊, and 26.70 ̊ are seen for GQDs, B-GQDs, and N-GQDs, respectively. The difference between the positions of the peaks can be due to the different sizes of boron and nitrogen atoms which can cause a change in their inter-layer distances. In particular, due to the larger radius of boron than carbon, the inter-layer distance is expected to increase, shifting the peak to lower angles. However, due to the smaller radius of nitrogen than carbon, the inter-layer distance is expected to decrease, shifting the peak to higher angles. TEM images of the prepared nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 3. The homogeneous and well-dispersed distribution of the nanoparticles is obvious in the TEM images. The size of the nanoparticles is within 2–5 nm with an average size of ∼3 nm. The elemental composition and distribution of the nanoparticle were also analyzed by the EDX mapping technique shown in Fig. 3. The type of elements and their distribution for each sample is clear in the pictures.

The energy bandgap of quantum dots depends on their size, shape, functional groups, and impurities. Here we studied the effect of boron and nitrogen dopants in GQDs on their bandgap by both electrochemical and optical methods. Regarding the electrochemical method, we used the CV technique to find their highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels. By applying a potential above the LUMO or below the HOMO levels versus vacuum (or a well-known reference such as Ag/AgCl), a cathodic or anodic current can be generated to detect the HOMO and LUMO levels. To this end, 5 μL of each sample combined with 0.5 μL of Nafion was dropped on a glassy carbon electrode and dried at room temperature. The anodic and cathodic currents measured by the CV method for each of the samples as well as for the bare glassy carbon electrode are shown in Fig. 4 . According to the following equations [15], the oxidation and reduction peaks associated with electron and hole injections were used to determine the HOMO and LUMO levels:
(1)
E HOMO
= − e
(
E
Ox , vs . NHE
+ 4.75
)
( ev )
(2)
E LUMO
= − e
(
E
red , vs . NHE
+ 4.75
)
( ev )
(3)
E NHE
=
E
AG / AgCl
+ 0.197
( eV )

As can be seen in Fig. 4, the working electrodes indicate reduction peaks at -0.71, -0.77, and -0.51 V and oxidation peaks at 2.28, 2.62, and 1.80 V versus Ag/AgCl (as a well-known reference) for GQDs, B-GQDs, and N-GQDs, respectively. Accordingly, ELUMO levels of -4.24, -4.18, and -4.44 eV and EHOMO levels of -7.23, -7.37, and -6.75 eV versus vacuum were calculated for GQDs, B-GQDs, and N-GQDs, respectively. Since the bandgap is the difference between ELUMO and EHOMO, the bandgaps of 2.99, 3.19, and 2.31 eV were calculated for GQDs, B-GQDs, and N-GQDs, respectively.

Fig. 5 shows UV-Vis absorption spectra and corresponding Tauc plots of the prepared nanoparticles [1]. The peaks at 228 nm for GQDs, 216 nm for B-GQDs, and 240 nm for N-GQDs in Fig. 5 (A) refer to their sp2 hybridized structures resulting from C-C and C=C bonds, and the peaks at 283 nm for GQDs, 272 nm for B-GQDs, and 369 nm for N-GQDs refer to their sp3 hybridized structures resulting from C=O bonds due to the n-π* transition [19]. There is another peak at 640 nm in the spectrum of N-GQDs, with a considerable absorption in the near-IR region that is attributed to the formation of extensive partial conjugated π electrons because of the average size of the N-GQDs about 5 nm [20]. The relation between π electrons and large macromolecular domains with the NIR absorption mechanism is observed in graphene oxide and has been reported in other investigations [21,22].The blue shift in the absorption peaks of B-GQDs could be due to completely empty π orbitals when creating a bond in the structure of GQDs, which ultimately leads to a reduction in free bonds and thus a reduction in the intermediate energy levels. The optical band gap (Eg) can be calculated using the Tauc's relation:
(4)
(
α hv
)
n
= B
(
h v
−
E g
)
where α is the absorption coefficient, ν is the photon frequency, n is a constant that depends on the bandgap type (1/2 and 2 for direct and indirect band gaps), B is a constant, h is the Planck's constant, and Eg is the optical bandgap [23]. Tauc plots of the samples are shown in Fig. 5 (B-D). The optical bandgaps of the prepared GQDs, B-GQDs, and N-GQDs were calculated 3.7, 3.83, and 1.75 eV, respectively. The smaller optical bandgap of N-GQDs is quite reasonable due to its absorption in the visible region, which is also consistent with the bandgap calculated by the CV method (discussed above).

Electrochemical performances of the samples were tested in a three-electrode setup containing 3 M KOH as electrolyte. Fig. 6 shows the CV curves of the samples measured within the potential window of 0-0.5 V at different scan rates. The typical peaks in Fig. 6 (A) are due to the occurrence of faradaic redox reactions with oxidation (0.29 and 0.32 V) and reduction peaks (0.25 and 0.15 V) of GQDs. The location of the peaks changes by both boron and nitrogen doping in GQDs, as seen in Fig. 6 (B, C). The peaks are located at 1.8 and 2.9 V in B-GQDs and at 1.2, 2.6, and 3.4 V in N-GQDs. The location of the peaks changes also by increasing the scan rate. It is also seen in Fig. 5 (A-C) that as the scan rate increases, the oxidation and reduction peaks shift to higher and lower potentials, respectively, which is because a shorter time would be available for the electrolyte ions to access the electrode's surface and the effective active area is out of reach for the electrolyte. The CV curves of the samples are shown in Fig. 6 (D) at the scan rate of 5 mV/s. As it is seen, the N-GQDs sample has a larger enclosed area than the other samples, leading to a higher specific capacity. The specific capacitance of the N-GQDs sample is 283 F/g at 1 A/g thanks to the higher conductivity of N-GQDs. By doping nitrogen in GQDs, due to the hybridization of the pz orbitals of N and C atoms, Л bonds are created in N-GQDs [18]. Since the formation of a Л bond requires lower energy than a σ bond, Л bonds will have a significant role in the decrease of the bandgap as compared to that of GQDs [17]. In addition to analyzing the effect of bandgap on the performance of nitrogen and boron on the ultimate efficiency of graphene quantum dots regarding the electrochemical activity, another reason that can justify the more effective role of nitrogen as compared to boron is the locations of these elements in the periodic table. These two elements, which are neighbors of carbon in the periodic table of elements, cause positive and negative changes in the chemical activity of the structure of graphene quantum dots. Nitrogen with its higher electronegativity than carbon and boron can increase the electrochemical activity and can attract electrons from the electrolyte, enhancing the electrochemical efficiency. Boron, on the other hand, plays an opposite role with its lower electronegativity. Fig. 7 (A-C) shows the GCD curves of the samples at different current densities in the potential window of 0–0.4 V. It is seen that as the current density increases, the charge-discharge time decreases, which may be due to the shorter time to perform faradaic processes on the electrode's surface that makes the electrode's surface unavailable for electrolyte ions. As shown in Fig. 7 (D), the discharge time of the N-GQDs electrode is longer than those of the other electrodes, discussed above. The specific capacitances of the samples were calculated from their discharge curves according to the following equation:
(5)
Csp = 2 | ∫ Vdt / m
Δ
v
2
where Csp, ∫Vdt, I/m, and ΔV are the specific capacitance (F/g), the area under the discharge curve, the current density (A/g), and the active potential window, respectively [24]. The specific capacitances of the samples are reported in Table 1 .

To calculate the specific energy and specific power of the prepared GQDs, B-GQDs, and N-GQDs, their specific capacitance values were calculated from their (GCD curves using the following equations [25,26]:
(6)
E = Csp
(
Δ
v
)
2
/ 2
(7)
P = E / t
where Csp, ΔV, and t are the specific capacitance (F/g), the operating voltage window (V), and the discharge time (s), respectively [3]. The specific energy versus specific power plots of the samples at the current density of 1 A/g are shown in Fig. 8 (B), and their values are listed in Table 3. The cycling stabilities of the samples were studied at the current density of 20 A/g for 2000 GCD cycles, as shown in Fig. 8 (A). It is seen that the cycling stability of N-GQDs is better than the other samples with high capacitance retention of 80% at 20 A/g after 2000 cycles. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to explore the electrode/electrolyte interfacial processes. The characteristics of charge transport and diffusion ions at the interface were obtained from the complex impedance plot known as the Nyquist plot [1].A typical Nyquist plot consists of three regions: (1) the high-frequency region, the x-intercept at the beginning of the semicircle on the Z-real axis, representing the equivalent series resistance (ESR), including the electrolyte resistance, the contact resistance at the interface between the electro-active material and the substrate, and the intrinsic resistance of the electro-active material [27], (2) the semi-circle in the middle-high-frequency region related to the charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the electrode/electrolyte interface [28,29], and (3) the inclined line in the low-frequency region, indicating the ions transport phenomena inside the electrode materials (the Warburg impedance) [25,28,30]. Fig. 8 (C) shows the Nyquist plots of the samples. In Fig. 8(C), we can observe that ESR, Rct, and the Warburg impedance of N-GQDs are lower than those of B-GQDs and GQDs. Therefore, the N-GQDs electrode has a higher conductivity suitable for a better charge transport of electrolyte ions, indicating the near-ideal capacitive behavior for supercapacitance, discussed in the previous section. Furthermore, all of the electrode materials offer good charge transfer kinetics due to their shorter diffusion path lengths [31,32]. A comparison table (Table 2
) for all different electrode materials with GQDs were mentioned below by giving the brief description of specific capacitance at different current density or scan rate.

Next, we assembled the N-GQDs//AC asymmetric cell with a pseudocapacitive-behaving cathode (N-GQDs) and a double-layer capacitive anode (AC) to demonstrate the real-world application of the electrode material. In an asymmetric setup, the mass balance between the positive and negative electrodes is calculated by the following equation [36]:
(8)
m + m =
C
S
−
Δ
V
−
/
C
S
+
Δ
V
+
where m, C, and ΔV denote the specific capacitance, the potential window, and the mass, respectively, for each of positive (+) and negative (–) electrodes. Fig. 9 (A) shows the CV curves of the asymmetric setup for different potential windows from 0.5 V to 1.7 V at the scan rate of 20 mV/s, showing quasi-rectangular shapes that illustrate the good electrochemical reversibility of the device. Fig. 9 (B) shows the GCD curves of the device for different current densities of 1, 2, and 3 A/g. The charge and discharge parts of the GCD curves are almost symmetric, which demonstrates a small internal resistance drop, indicating the contributions from both the faradaic processes and the double-layer capacitance. The real-world application of the setup we demonstrated by powering a red LED, as shown in Fig. 9 (C).

In summary, we successfully synthesized GQDs, B-GQDs, and N-GQDs nanoparticles via a facile hydrothermal method. Among them, the N-GQDs nanoparticles exhibited the highest specific capacitance of 283 F/g at 1 A/g, which is attributed to its higher electrical conductivity and electronegativity than those of GQDs and B-GQDs. The specific energy and specific power of the N-GQDs electrode were obtained 6.2 Wh/Kg and 108 W/Kg, respectively. Furthermore, the N-GQDs electrode exhibited better cyclic stability than the other samples with an 80% capacity retention after 2000 charge-discharge cycles. The results suggest that the synthesized N-GQDs have great potential for supercapacitors.

Mohamad Taghi Dejpasand: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Samira Sharifi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Esmaiel Saievar-Iranizad: Supervision. Ahmad Yazdani: Writing – review & editing. Kourosh Rahimi: Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An efficient pseudocapacitor electrode material with co-doping of iron (II) and sulfur in luminescent graphene quantum dots
Diam. Relat. Mater.
